---
title: "The operator algebra: sparse maps and semirings"
---

The previous chapter described a geometric computation as a finite
approximation to a continuous claim. This chapter describes the algebraic
substrate that makes those computations reusable. The recurring pattern is
simple:

1. for each output location, gather a small set of neighbours;
2. combine each neighbour's value with an edge value;
3. reduce the resulting messages.

The neighbour set may come from a mesh, a graph, a regular grid stencil, a
spherical template, or a cross-level pooling map. The reduction may be ordinary
addition, a maximum, a minimum, a log-sum-exp, or a boolean disjunction. The
central design choice in `nitrix` is to separate these two questions. Storage
answers: *which neighbours are present?* A semiring answers: *what arithmetic is
used to combine them?*

This separation is more than an implementation convenience. It is a way of
putting geometric bias into code, in the precise sense of
[the previous chapter](foundations.qmd#sec-transformations-bias). A mesh operator
should not depend on the accidental order in which vertices are stored - it
should be permutation equivariant. A spherical operator should use
neighbourhoods on the sphere, not on a flattened latitude-longitude image. A
morphological dilation and a smoothing step may use the same local window, but
they express different assumptions about what information should propagate. The
operator algebra lets these assumptions be stated explicitly, and reused.

## Sparse Neighbour Maps {#sec-neighbours}

Let $x_i$ be a feature vector at vertex, voxel, or graph node $i$. A local
operator computes an output at row $i$ from a set of neighbours
$\mathcal{N}(i)$:

$$
y_i = \operatorname{reduce}_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)}
      \operatorname{message}(i,j,x_i,x_j).
$$

In ordinary linear algebra this is a sparse matrix-vector product. If the
operator is $A$, then

$$
y_i = \sum_j A_{ij}x_j,
$$

where $A_{ij}=0$ for almost all pairs. The dense matrix is the wrong object to
store. A cortical surface with hundreds of thousands of vertices has only a
handful of neighbours per vertex, so a dense $n\times n$ matrix would be roughly
$10^{11}$ entries, almost all of them zero. The meaningful object is the sparse
neighbour map: for each row, the columns it can see and the values on those
edges.

Geometric sparsity is not arbitrary sparsity. It is sparse *but structured*: not
a random scatter of non-zeros, but roughly the same small number per row. Regular
grids have fixed stencil widths. Icospheres have valence nearly always six.
Cortical meshes have mostly small valence with occasional irregular vertices.
This near-uniform row length is the property the storage format will exploit, and
the reason `nitrix` uses ELL-style storage rather than a fully general sparse
format as its primary representation.

## ELL Storage {#sec-ell}

The ELL format (the name is historical, from ELLPACK) stores a sparse operator as
two dense rectangular arrays:

- `values`, shape `(m, k_max)`;
- `indices`, shape `(m, k_max)`.

Here $m$ is the number of output rows and $k_{\max}$ is the maximum number of
stored entries in any row. The entry `indices[i, p]` says which input row is the
$p$th neighbour of output row $i$, and `values[i, p]` is the corresponding edge
value. The `ELL` container also records `n_cols`, the size of the input axis,
and `identity`, the pad value used in `values`.

The price of the rectangle is padding. If row $i$ has only $k_i < k_{\max}$
neighbours, the remaining slots must be no-ops, and a pad slot has two
requirements. Its **index** must be in range, so the gather cannot read out of
bounds - which input row it points at is irrelevant, only that it is valid. Its
**value** must make the gathered operand disappear under the chosen algebra. For
the shipped algebras except `EUCLIDEAN`, that value is the semiring identity
recorded on the `ELL`: the element the reduction leaves untouched, so a padded
term contributes nothing.

For a dense operand $X\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{\text{cols}}\times d}$, the ELL
contraction is

$$
Y_{i\ell}
  =
  \bigoplus_{p=0}^{k_{\max}-1}
  \left(
    \operatorname{values}_{ip}
    \otimes
    X_{\operatorname{indices}_{ip},\,\ell}
  \right).
$$ {#eq-operators-ellmatvec}

The symbols $\oplus$ and $\otimes$ are deliberately not fixed yet. In ordinary
matrix multiplication they are $+$ and $\times$. In a dilation they are $\max$
and $+$. The loop structure is unchanged: $\operatorname{indices}[i,p]$ says
*which* neighbour, $\operatorname{values}[i,p]$ says *how much*, the gather
fetches the neighbour's value, the combine $\otimes$ weights it, and the reduce
$\oplus$ folds the $k_{\max}$ terms into one.

This is why ELL is attractive for JAX/XLA. The computation is a fixed
rectangular loop over the $k_{\max}$ axis - a cost of $O(m\,k_{\max})$ with no
data-dependent inner-loop length, no row pointer to interpret at runtime, and no
irregular Python object inside the traced computation. `ELL` itself is a JAX
pytree: `values` and `indices` are array children, while `n_cols` and `identity`
are static metadata. Gradients flow through `values` and the dense operand;
integer `indices` are topology and have zero/`float0` cotangents - the algebraic
expression of the rule that you differentiate the weights, never the
neighbourhood structure.

### Why Not CSR or BCOO?

A reader from numerical linear algebra will know **CSR** (compressed sparse row:
all non-zeros in flat arrays plus per-row pointers) and may know JAX's **BCOO**
(batched coordinate format). Both are more general than ELL: they store genuinely
ragged patterns with no padding waste. That generality is not free. The number of
entries in row $i$ is data-dependent, so a matvec needs either dynamic inner
loops, segmented reductions, or a more complex lowering - all awkward under `jit`
and hard to keep differentiable with a clean custom gradient.

ELL makes the opposite trade. It spends some memory on padding to make the hot
path a static dense-array program: two ordinary `(m, k_max)` tensors that flow
through `jit`, `vmap`, and `grad` with no special handling and no dependency on
`jax.experimental.sparse`. The bet, recorded in the design notes
[`sparse-specialisations.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md),
is that neuroimaging geometry usually has enough structure for the padding to be
cheap and the compile-autodiff-batch story to be clean.

### The Padding Cliff and `SectionedELL`

ELL has an obvious failure mode, and it is worth seeing clearly. It pads *every*
row to the global $k_{\max}$. That is nearly free when degrees are uniform
($k_{\max}\approx$ the median degree, as on the icosphere). It becomes
pathological when one row is much longer than the rest - a single high-valence
hub, or a distance-thresholded neighbourhood in an irregular point cloud where
$k_{\max}$ can be $10$-$100\times$ the median. Most of the rectangle is then
identity padding: memory and FLOPs spent on no-ops. This is the **padding
cliff**, and @fig-operators-ell shows it on a tiny graph.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-ell
#| fig-cap: "Left: a small irregular adjacency stored as a flat ELL. Every row is padded to the global k_max=6 set by the single hub vertex; real cells (blue) carry a neighbour index, padded cells (grey, 'id') carry the semiring identity and an in-range sentinel index. More than half the grid is wasted padding - the padding cliff. Right: the same adjacency as a SectionedELL - rows are bucketed by ceil(log2(degree)), each bucket gets its own small k_max, and the total stored cells drop sharply."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle

# A small irregular adjacency on 8 vertices: a line with one high-valence "hub".
adj = {
    0: [1],
    1: [0, 2],
    2: [1, 3],
    3: [2, 4],
    4: [3, 5, 6, 7, 1, 2],   # the hub
    5: [4],
    6: [4],
    7: [4],
}
n = len(adj)
k_max = max(len(v) for v in adj.values())   # global padding width

ell_idx = np.full((n, k_max), -1, dtype=int)   # -1 marks pad for drawing
real = np.zeros((n, k_max), dtype=bool)
for i in range(n):
    nb = adj[i]
    ell_idx[i, : len(nb)] = nb
    real[i, : len(nb)] = True

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4.8))

# ---- Left: flat ELL, the padding cliff ----
A = ax[0]
for i in range(n):
    for p in range(k_max):
        is_real = real[i, p]
        color = "#9ecae1" if is_real else "#f0f0f0"
        A.add_patch(Rectangle((p, n - 1 - i), 1, 1, facecolor=color,
                              edgecolor="0.4", lw=0.8))
        txt = str(ell_idx[i, p]) if is_real else "id"
        A.text(p + 0.5, n - 1 - i + 0.5, txt, ha="center", va="center",
               fontsize=9, color=("k" if is_real else "0.6"))
A.set_xlim(0, k_max); A.set_ylim(0, n)
A.set_xticks(np.arange(k_max) + 0.5); A.set_xticklabels(range(k_max))
A.set_yticks(np.arange(n) + 0.5); A.set_yticklabels(range(n - 1, -1, -1))
A.set_xlabel("slot p  (0 .. k_max-1)"); A.set_ylabel("row i (vertex)")
n_real = real.sum(); n_pad = real.size - n_real
A.set_title(f"flat ELL: (n, k_max)=({n},{k_max})\n"
            f"{n_real} real cells, {n_pad} identity-padded "
            f"({100 * n_pad / real.size:.0f}% wasted)")
A.set_aspect("equal")

# ---- Right: SectionedELL, rows bucketed by ceil(log2(degree)) ----
def bucket(d):
    return 0 if d <= 1 else (d - 1).bit_length()

buckets = {}
for i in range(n):
    buckets.setdefault(bucket(len(adj[i])), []).append(i)

B = ax[1]
y = 0
total_sectioned = 0
palette = ["#a1d99b", "#fdae6b", "#bcbddc", "#fa9fb5"]
for b in sorted(buckets):
    rows = buckets[b]
    bk = 1 if b == 0 else 2 ** b
    bk = max(bk, max(len(adj[i]) for i in rows))   # bucket-local k_max
    total_sectioned += len(rows) * bk
    for i in rows:
        nb = adj[i]
        for p in range(bk):
            is_real = p < len(nb)
            color = palette[b % len(palette)] if is_real else "#f0f0f0"
            B.add_patch(Rectangle((p, y), 1, 1, facecolor=color,
                                  edgecolor="0.4", lw=0.8))
            txt = str(nb[p]) if is_real else "id"
            B.text(p + 0.5, y + 0.5, txt, ha="center", va="center",
                   fontsize=9, color=("k" if is_real else "0.6"))
        B.text(-0.4, y + 0.5, f"v{i}", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=8)
        y += 1
    B.text(-1.7, y - len(rows) / 2, f"bucket {b}\nk={bk}", ha="center",
           va="center", fontsize=8, color="0.3")
    y += 0.4

B.set_xlim(-2.2, k_max); B.set_ylim(0, y)
B.set_xticks(np.arange(k_max) + 0.5); B.set_xticklabels(range(k_max))
B.set_yticks([])
B.set_xlabel("slot p (bucket-local)")
B.set_title(f"SectionedELL: rows bucketed by degree\n"
            f"{total_sectioned} stored cells vs {real.size} flat "
            f"({100 * total_sectioned / real.size:.0f}% of flat)")
B.set_aspect("equal")

fig.suptitle("ELL stores neighbour lists as a dense (n, k_max) pair; "
             "SectionedELL buckets rows by degree to dodge the padding cliff",
             y=1.02, fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

`SectionedELL` is the remedy: bucket the rows by degree, store one small ELL per
bucket, and scatter the results back. The bucketing is by $\lceil\log_2
\deg\rceil$ - degree 1 in bucket 0 ($k_{\max}=1$), degree 2 in bucket 1 ($k=2$),
degrees 3-4 in bucket 2 ($k=4$), degrees 5-8 in bucket 3 ($k=8$), and so on - so
a bucket's local width is the power of two that bounds its rows' degrees. Memory
is then dominated by the *largest bucket*, not by the worst-case row. Each section
is still an ELL, so each remains a rectangular JAX computation; only the global
outlier-driven padding is gone. The right half of @fig-operators-ell shows the
hub landing alone in a wide bucket while everyone else sits in narrow ones.

This is a clean example of the execution classes from
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-classes). Building a `SectionedELL` is
`HOST-CTOR`: the row degrees and bucket shapes are data-dependent. Applying an
already-built `SectionedELL` is `DIFF-JAX`: the sections are fixed pytrees and the
per-section matvecs can be traced and reused. You pay the host cost once per
adjacency, then run the operator inside `jit`-ed code many times - exactly the
discipline of never calling a `HOST` op inside a jitted loop. The storage
economics, alignment, and tiling live in
[performance](performance.qmd#sec-storage).

### Construction Helpers

The low-level helpers in `nitrix.sparse` are small but important; all are noted
`HOST-CTOR` or `DIFF-JAX`.

- `ell_pad(values, indices, *, k_max, n_cols, identity=0.0, pad_index=0)`
  (`HOST-CTOR`) pads already-rectangular per-row arrays to a requested width,
  filling the trailing slots of `values` with `identity` and of `indices` with
  `pad_index` (which it checks lies in range).
- `ell_from_dense(dense, *, k_max=None, threshold=0.0, identity=0.0,
  symmetrize=False)` (`HOST-CTOR`) builds an ELL from a dense matrix by keeping
  the largest-magnitude entries per row - a convenience for tests and small
  problems. One sharp edge: top-$k$-per-row selection is **not symmetry
  preserving** (row $i$ may keep edge $(i,j)$ while row $j$ drops $(j,i)$), so the
  stored pattern is generally asymmetric even for a symmetric input. That matters
  for spectral consumers whose iterative solvers assume a symmetric operator.
  Either pass `symmetrize=True` to store $\tfrac12(S+S^\top)$ (but a hub selected
  by every row can densify its row), or use the storage-free matvec
  symmetrisation below.
- `ell_to_dense(ell)` (`DIFF-JAX`) scatters an ELL back to a dense matrix via
  `scatter_add`, mainly for tests and fallback paths. Because the scatter is
  additive, round-trip correctness holds for `REAL`; do not rely on it under a
  non-additive algebra.
- `ell_mask(ell, valid, *, semiring=...)` (`DIFF-JAX`) removes edges that reach a
  masked vertex or voxel - the cortical **medial wall**, or a grey-matter mask. A
  masked edge is a true no-op only if its value is the algebra's
  **$\otimes$-annihilator**: the element $z$ with $z\otimes b$ equal to the
  additive identity for *every* $b$, read from `semiring.annihilator`. For most
  algebras the annihilator coincides with the additive identity ($0$ for `REAL`,
  $-\infty$ for `LOG` and max-plus, $+\infty$ for min-plus), but `EUCLIDEAN` is
  the exception: its combine $(a-b)^2$ has no annihilator - there is no $z$ for
  which $(z-b)^2$ vanishes for every $b$ - so it cannot be value-masked and
  `ell_mask(semiring=EUCLIDEAN)` raises; drop the columns structurally instead.
- `ell_add_self_loops(ell, ...)` (`DIFF-JAX`) appends an $(i,i)$ edge to each row,
  growing $k_{\max}$ by one. This is a graph-neural-network construction (the GCN
  renormalisation $\hat A = A + I$; GAT attending each node to itself), **not**
  part of geometric mesh adjacency - `mesh_k_ring_adjacency` is self-loop-free,
  because the centre weight is the Laplacian's job.

The discipline that ties these together is to distinguish construction from use.
Helpers that inspect ragged Python lists, dense matrices, or data-dependent row
degrees are host construction. The resulting ELL-like object is what belongs in
the differentiable computation.

## The Semiring Abstraction {#sec-semiring}

Equation @eq-operators-ellmatvec contains two operations: a **combine** $\otimes$
that weights a gathered neighbour, and a **reduce** $\oplus$ that folds the
weighted terms into one number. In ordinary sparse matvec these are fixed -
$\otimes$ is multiplication, $\oplus$ is addition. The organizing idea of this
chapter is to make them *parameters*. The gather kernel does not depend on which
pair you choose: swap the algebra, keep the kernel, and the same neighbour map
computes a different geometric quantity. **Change the algebra, not the kernel.**

In `nitrix`, a `Semiring` packages these choices as a first-class object:

- a `monoid`, the reduce, with the four hooks a streaming reduction needs
  (`init`, `update`, `merge`, `finalize`);
- a `binary_op`, the combine (a `Semigroup` with a single `combine` method);
- an `identity`, the value a padded slot carries to vanish under the reduce;
- an `annihilator`, used by `ell_mask` when an edge must become a true no-op;
- a `name` and the custom VJP rules for dense and ELL contractions.

The usual algebraic laws are useful but not always required by the kernel. A
`StrictSemiring` declares the stronger laws (associativity and distributivity)
needed by a reduction that may reorder or re-associate terms, such as a
tree/cross-device reduction. A relaxed `Semiring` is enough when the kernel fixes
the order of the loop. The split is a *signed declaration*, not a runtime check;
`as_strict()` / `as_relaxed()` only re-tag. `EUCLIDEAN` is deliberately relaxed:
its combine is not associative and it applies a non-monoidal $\sqrt{\cdot}$
projection only at the end.

The built-in algebras are:

| Algebra | Combine $\otimes$ | Reduce $\oplus$ | Identity | Typical geometry |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| `REAL` | $a b$ | $\sum$ | $0$ | weighted averages, diffusion, smoothing |
| `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` | $a+b$ | $\max$ | $-\infty$ | dilation, max-pooling |
| `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` | $a+b$ | $\min$ | $+\infty$ | erosion, shortest paths, distance transforms |
| `LOG` | $a+b$ | $\operatorname{logsumexp}$ | $-\infty$ | soft aggregation, marginalisation |
| `BOOLEAN` | $a\wedge b$ | $\vee$ | `False` | reachability and connectivity |
| `EUCLIDEAN` | $(a-b)^2$ | $\sqrt{\sum}$ | $0$ | local Euclidean neighbour distance |

Read the first three rows as the spine of the unit. `REAL` $(+,\times)$ is the
ordinary sparse matvec: one step of diffusion, the weighted average behind mesh
smoothing and the cotangent-Laplacian apply. The **tropical** algebras are the
algebra of dynamic programming: max-plus $(\max,+)$ is morphological **dilation**
(combine by adding the structuring weight, reduce by the max); min-plus
$(\min,+)$ is **erosion** and, iterated to a fixed point, the **distance
transform** and single-source shortest path. The other three fill out the
surface: `LOG` replaces the hard $\max$ with a soft, numerically stable
`logsumexp` (the flash-attention softmax trick); `BOOLEAN` $(\wedge,\vee)$ is pure
reachability; `EUCLIDEAN` measures $\ell_2$ neighbour distance. You can also
compose your own algebra from any `Monoid` and `Semigroup`; the kernels do not
care whether it is built in.

This is the geometric-learning point in algebraic form. The neighbourhood encodes
which interactions are allowed - the inductive bias of
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-transformations-bias) - and the semiring encodes
the kind of evidence propagation. A graph neural network, a mesh smoother, a
morphological operator, and a distance transform can all share one gather skeleton
while making different assumptions about how local information should compose.

### One Operator, Three Algebras

Consider a one-dimensional path graph: each vertex is connected to itself and its
immediate neighbours. The storage is fixed - every row gathers the same
three-point window - so the only thing that changes between the panels of
@fig-operators-semiring is the pair $(\oplus,\otimes)$. Under `REAL` with weights
$(\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)$ the operator is a smoothing step,
$$
y_i = \tfrac{1}{3}\left(x_{i-1}+x_i+x_{i+1}\right);
$$
under max-plus with flat (zero) structuring weights the same indices compute a
dilation, $y_i=\max\{x_{i-1},x_i,x_{i+1}\}$; under min-plus, an erosion,
$y_i=\min\{x_{i-1},x_i,x_{i+1}\}$. Nothing about the neighbourhood changed; the
inductive bias changed because the algebra changed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-semiring
#| fig-cap: "One ELL operator, three semirings, one input signal. The path-graph adjacency and the gather-and-reduce loop are identical across panels; only (reduce, combine) changes. REAL (+,x) with row-normalised weights gives a weighted average (a diffusion/smoothing step). max-plus (max,+) with a flat structuring element takes the max over each window (grayscale dilation - the bump widens and the notch fills). min-plus (min,+) takes the min (erosion - the bump shrinks)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A path graph on 11 vertices, stored as a fixed (n, k_max=3) ELL: each row's
# indices are its three neighbours (endpoints clamp to themselves).
n = 11
idx = np.zeros((n, 3), dtype=int)
for i in range(n):
    idx[i] = [max(i - 1, 0), i, min(i + 1, n - 1)]

# A toy per-vertex signal: a single bump plus a small notch.
B = np.zeros(n)
B[3:6] = 1.0
B[7] = 0.4

def ell_matmul(values, indices, b, combine, reduce_, identity):
    # C[i] = reduce_p ( values[i, p] combine b[indices[i, p]] ) -- the SAME loop
    # for every algebra; only `combine`, `reduce_`, and `identity` change.
    out = np.full(n, identity, dtype=float)
    for i in range(n):
        acc = identity
        for p in range(indices.shape[1]):
            acc = reduce_(acc, combine(values[i, p], b[indices[i, p]]))
        out[i] = acc
    return out

# REAL (+, x): a row-normalised average -> one diffusion / smoothing step.
val_real = np.full((n, 3), 1.0 / 3.0)
out_real = ell_matmul(val_real, idx, B,
                      combine=lambda v, x: v * x,
                      reduce_=lambda a, b: a + b, identity=0.0)

# max-plus (max, +): a flat structuring element -> grayscale DILATION.
val_struct = np.zeros((n, 3))   # 0 is the additive identity for the (+) combine
out_max = ell_matmul(val_struct, idx, B,
                     combine=lambda v, x: v + x,
                     reduce_=lambda a, b: max(a, b), identity=-np.inf)

# min-plus (min, +): same structuring element -> grayscale EROSION.
out_min = ell_matmul(val_struct, idx, B,
                     combine=lambda v, x: v + x,
                     reduce_=lambda a, b: min(a, b), identity=+np.inf)

x = np.arange(n)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 3.8), sharey=True)
for a in ax:
    a.step(x, B, where="mid", color="0.6", lw=1.5, label="input $B$")
    a.set_xlabel("vertex i"); a.set_ylim(-0.15, 1.25); a.grid(alpha=0.25)

ax[0].step(x, out_real, where="mid", color="C0", lw=2.4, label="REAL $(+,\\times)$")
ax[0].set_title("REAL: weighted average\n(diffusion / smoothing)")
ax[0].set_ylabel("value"); ax[0].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
ax[1].step(x, out_max, where="mid", color="C3", lw=2.4, label="max-plus $(\\max,+)$")
ax[1].set_title("max-plus: max over window\n(dilation)")
ax[1].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
ax[2].step(x, out_min, where="mid", color="C2", lw=2.4, label="min-plus $(\\min,+)$")
ax[2].set_title("min-plus: min over window\n(erosion)")
ax[2].legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("One ELL operator, three semirings: swap $(\\oplus,\\otimes)$, "
             "change the geometry", y=1.03)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The example is small enough to seem trivial, but it is exactly why the substrate
is useful. At cortical scale, at spherical-template scale, and on volumetric
stencils it is still the same sentence: gather local neighbours and reduce them in
the algebra that matches the geometry of the operation.

## Kernels and Differentiability

The public reductions are all `DIFF-JAX`:

- `semiring_ell_matmul(values, indices, B, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols=None,
  backend='auto')`, the ELL contraction in @eq-operators-ellmatvec and the
  load-bearing kernel of the unit. It is a `jax.custom_vjp` whose backward routes
  to the algebra's rule; leading axes broadcast via `vmap`. The `backend` selects
  `'jax'` (always available) or `'pallas-cuda'` (an opt-in Triton kernel that
  falls back to JAX, with one warning, when it cannot tile a shape - see
  [`ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md)).
- `semiring_matmul(A, B, *, semiring=REAL, backend='auto')`, the *dense* analogue
  $C_{ij}=\bigoplus_k (A_{ik}\otimes B_{kj})$ for small or dense operands. For
  `REAL` on real inputs it equals `A @ B`, so call `jnp.matmul` there; the value
  of `semiring_matmul` is the *other* algebras.
- `semiring_ell_rmatvec(values, indices, X, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols=...)`, the
  **adjoint** $Y = A^\top X$. Where the matvec *gathers* (each output row pulls
  from its neighbours), the rmatvec *scatters* (each row adds its contribution
  into the columns it points at). It is `REAL`-only, because the additive scatter
  needs linear structure; a non-additive algebra has no meaningful "average of
  $A$ and $A^\top$". Its main use is the storage-free symmetric matvec
  $$
  x \mapsto \tfrac12\!\left(Ax + A^\top x\right),
  $$
  which a spectral solver needs when a top-$k$ construction (e.g.
  `ell_from_dense`) did not preserve symmetry.

The forward implementations have pure-JAX reference cores in
`semiring._reference`, the correctness floor every accelerated path is checked
against - the validation-by-reference posture of
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-validation): agreement with the reference
within tolerance, not bit-parity across backends.

Every built-in algebra carries a hand-derived `jax.custom_vjp`, so `jax.grad` /
`jacrev` return finite-difference-checked gradients with no further setup. `REAL`
has the familiar transpose-matvec backward. `LOG` has a softmax-weighted backward.
The tropical algebras are the subtle ones: $\max$ and $\min$ are non-smooth, so
their backward is the **subgradient** - a one-hot gather that routes the entire
cotangent to the single arg-max or arg-min neighbour (a winner-take-all rule that
is the correct subgradient of a max). `EUCLIDEAN` uses a normalised-difference
rule with a guard near zero. The integer `indices` are never differentiated: they
are the discrete neighbourhood, and changing them is a different combinatorial
problem. The depth of these rules lives in [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-kernels).

### Functional Edge Messages

The kernels above reduce *stored* edge values. Some models want the edge message
computed on the fly from the source feature, the neighbour feature, the stored
value, the index pair, and optional edge attributes - the graph-neural-network
pattern. That is

```python
semiring_ell_edge_aggregate(edge_fn, ell, x, *, semiring=REAL, edge_attr=None)
```

(`DIFF-JAX`): it forms $e[i,p]=\texttt{edge\_fn}(x_i, x_{\text{indices}[i,p]},
\text{values}[i,p], (i,j)[, a])$ for each edge, then reduces $\bigoplus_p e[i,p]$
under the algebra. The reduce half is identical to `semiring_ell_matmul`; only
the value-of-edge step is opened up. This is the substrate for EdgeConv/DGCNN,
GAT, MoNet, and GCN messages without rewriting the gather/reduce kernel per model
family. (First-cut support is `REAL` / `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` / `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` -
the single-axis-reduction monoids; `LOG` and `EUCLIDEAN` raise. The user's
`edge_fn` is responsible for letting pad slots contribute the identity, typically
by multiplying the message by `values`, which is $0$ at `REAL` pads.)

`ell_row_softmax(scores, ell)` (`DIFF-JAX`) is the companion attention pre-pass:
it normalises per-edge scores along the $k_{\max}$ axis so each row sums to one,
forcing padding slots to $-\infty$ before the softmax (an all-pad row returns
all-zeros, not `NaN`). Score the edges, `ell_row_softmax` them into weights, feed
the weights back as the `values` of a `REAL` edge-aggregate, and you have
GAT/GATv2 attention. The regular-grid sibling of the whole machine is
`semiring_conv` - the same gather-and-reduce over a shift stencil rather than a
neighbour list - which is the substrate the volumetric morphology of
[volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology) is built on.

## The Apply Seam {#sec-apply}

Downstream geometry code should not branch on sparse storage. It should not have
one curvature implementation for flat ELL and another for `SectionedELL`. The
format boundary is absorbed by

```python
apply_operator(op, x, *, semiring=None, backend="jax")
```

(`DIFF-JAX`), where `op` is an `ELL` or `SectionedELL` and `x` has shape
`(..., n, d)` (use a trailing singleton `(..., n, 1)` for a scalar field).
Leading axes are treated as batch axes and the operator is shared across them, not
itself batched. If `op` is an `ELL`, `apply_operator` calls `semiring_ell_matmul`;
if it is a `SectionedELL`, it calls `sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul`, applies each
section, and scatters rows back into their original order. The result has shape
`(..., m, d)`.

The line worth carrying forward is **operators versus algorithms**: assembly
downstairs, composition upstairs. The dependency runs one way, `geometry →
sparse`. The geometry layer *assembles* an operator - a cotangent Laplacian, a
k-ring adjacency, an icosphere pooling map - and hands it to the sparse layer,
which *applies* it. Because `apply_operator` hides the storage format, a flat
`ELL` (the near-uniform icosphere) and a `SectionedELL` (a `recon-all` white
surface with a few high-valence vertices) flow through identical algorithm code:
the curvature, distortion, and geodesic-smoothing routines never branch on
storage. `SectionedELL` carries its own apply pair -
`sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul` and the `REAL`-only adjoint
`sectioned_semiring_ell_rmatvec` - which compose to the same symmetric matvec on
bucketed adjacencies.

This is also where the codebase enforces a clean separation:

- geometry routines construct operators with geometric meaning;
- sparse routines apply operators without knowing that meaning;
- semirings choose the arithmetic of local propagation.

A spherical registration algorithm can build one adjacency, pass it through
`apply_operator`, and remain agnostic to whether the source mesh was a nearly
regular icosphere or an irregular subject-specific surface. The operator is the
same mathematical object; the storage is an implementation detail.

## Adjacency and Measures {#sec-adjacency}

Everything above needed an operator to apply. This section is where surface
operators come from. The unifying statement, which sets up the next chapter:

> A mesh is a **graph** - the faces tell you who neighbours whom - plus a
> **metric** - the vertex positions give you the weights.

The graph half is pure topology (face indices); the metric half is geometry
(vertex coordinates). @fig-operators-adjacency shows both halves on a level-1
icosphere. The [cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd) on the next page is
exactly the graph adjacency reweighted by a metric quantity (the cotangent edge
weights), and the mass matrix is the vertex-area diagonal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-operators-adjacency
#| fig-cap: "A mesh is a graph plus a metric, on a level-1 icosphere (42 vertices). Left: the combinatorics - each vertex coloured by its 1-ring degree (valence). Twelve vertices inherit valence 5 from the icosahedron's corners; the rest are valence 6. This is the graph the adjacency operator encodes. Right: the metric - each vertex coloured by its barycentric area A_i, the per-vertex measure the area-weighted operators use. The graph says who-neighbours-whom; the metric says how-much-each-weighs."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Build a level-1 icosphere by hand (icosahedron + one subdivision).
phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
V = np.array([
    [-1, phi, 0], [1, phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
    [0, -1, phi], [0, 1, phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
    [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
], dtype=float)
V /= np.linalg.norm(V, axis=1, keepdims=True)
F = np.array([
    [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
    [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
    [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
    [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
], dtype=int)

def subdivide(V, F):
    cache, Vl = {}, V.tolist()
    def mid(i, j):
        key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (np.array(Vl[i]) + np.array(Vl[j])) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
        Vl.append(m.tolist()); cache[key] = len(Vl) - 1
        return cache[key]
    NF = []
    for a, b, c in F:
        ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
        NF += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.array(Vl), np.array(NF)

V, F = subdivide(V, F)   # level-1 icosphere: 42 vertices
n = len(V)

# The COMBINATORICS: 1-ring degree (who neighbours whom).
adj = [set() for _ in range(n)]
for a, b, c in F:
    for i, j in [(a, b), (b, c), (c, a)]:
        adj[i].add(j); adj[j].add(i)
degree = np.array([len(s) for s in adj])

# The METRIC: per-vertex (barycentric) area.
v0, v1, v2 = V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 1]], V[F[:, 2]]
face_area = 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(v1 - v0, v2 - v0), axis=1)
vert_area = np.zeros(n)
for k in range(3):
    np.add.at(vert_area, F[:, k], face_area / 3.0)

X, Y = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]
order = np.argsort(V[:, 2])   # painter's order, back to front
edges = set()
for a, b, c in F:
    for i, j in [(a, b), (b, c), (c, a)]:
        edges.add((min(i, j), max(i, j)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5.2))
for A, vals, title, cmap, lbl in [
    (ax[0], degree, "combinatorics: 1-ring degree\n(the GRAPH: who neighbours whom)",
     "viridis", "valence (5 or 6)"),
    (ax[1], vert_area, "metric: per-vertex area $A_i$\n(the WEIGHTS the operators use)",
     "magma", "vertex area"),
]:
    for i, j in edges:
        A.plot([X[i], X[j]], [Y[i], Y[j]], color="0.8", lw=0.5, zorder=1)
    sc = A.scatter(X[order], Y[order], c=vals[order], cmap=cmap,
                   s=90, edgecolor="k", lw=0.4, zorder=2)
    A.set_title(title); A.set_aspect("equal"); A.set_xticks([]); A.set_yticks([])
    fig.colorbar(sc, ax=A, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04, label=lbl)

n5 = int((degree == 5).sum())
fig.suptitle(f"A mesh is a GRAPH plus a METRIC. Level-1 icosphere: {n} vertices, "
             f"{n5} have valence 5 (the icosahedron's corners), the rest valence 6",
             y=1.0, fontsize=10.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The mesh container is `Mesh(vertices, faces)`, with `vertices` of shape `(n, 3)`
and `faces` of shape `(m, 3)`. It is a pytree; gradients flow through the
floating-point vertices, and integer faces are topology. The canonical analytic
mesh is `icosphere(n_iterations)`, the refinement sequence of @sec-discretization;
`icosphere_hierarchy(max_level)` records that sequence together with parent tables
and is *not* a pytree (it is host-side construction metadata). The hierarchy
exists so host constructors can emit concrete cross-level ELL operators -
`icosphere_cross_level_adjacency` (a coarse$\to$fine pooling adjacency) and
`icosphere_bary_upsampler` (a fine-from-coarse barycentric upsampler) - which are
then reusable inside JAX computations and feed the spherical pooling of
[graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd).

The **graph** half becomes an applyable operator through

```python
mesh_k_ring_adjacency(mesh, *, k=1, binary=True, include_self=False, format='ell')
```

(`HOST-CTOR`). It reads undirected edges from faces to get the 1-ring, then
BFS-expands $k$ steps to the $k$-ring. The flags set what the operator *means*:
`binary=True` stores a $1$ at each neighbour (a plain adjacency, the right thing
to reduce under a tropical algebra - count, dilate, reach); `binary=False` stores
$1/|\mathcal{N}(i)|$ per row (a row-stochastic random-walk operator, so a `REAL`
matvec is a neighbour average - one step of diffusion); `include_self` adds the
centre vertex (off by default - geometric adjacency is strictly the neighbours);
and `format` is `'ell'`, `'sectioned'`, or `'auto'`, where `'auto'` sections only
when the max/median degree ratio exceeds about $2$. Near-uniform template surfaces
(icosphere, `fsaverage`) stay flat; irregular-valence surfaces section. The return
type is therefore `ELL | SectionedELL` - exactly the difference `apply_operator`
exists to absorb.

The **metric** half is three differentiable, pure-JAX measures - all `DIFF-JAX`,
all grad-through-`vertices`, the hot-path ingredients of the area-weighted
operators on later pages.

- `face_areas(mesh)` returns $\tfrac12\lVert(v_1-v_0)\times(v_2-v_0)\rVert$ per
  face - half the magnitude of the edge cross product, the parallelogram-area fact
  from linear algebra.
- `vertex_areas(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi')` is the lumped mass diagonal $A_i$ that
  weights every area-aware operation ($M^{-1}L$ mean curvature, areal distortion,
  the smoothing solve $(M+tL)x=Mx_0$). `'barycentric'` gives each vertex
  $\text{area}/3$ - cheap, always non-negative. `'voronoi'` (default) is the
  mixed-Voronoi rule of Meyer et al. (2003): a non-obtuse triangle gives vertex
  $i$ its circumcentric-dual area, but an **obtuse** triangle - where the Voronoi
  cell falls outside the triangle - gives the obtuse vertex $\text{area}/2$ and
  the others $\text{area}/4$. Both satisfy $\sum_i A_i = \sum_f \text{area}_f$
  exactly. The obtuse branch is load-bearing on real cortical surfaces and *never*
  exercised by the icosphere - a reminder that an analytic oracle, perfect for
  convergence checks, does not stress every code path.
- `compute_vertex_normals(vertices, faces)` gives unit per-vertex normals. Each
  face contributes its un-normalised cross-product normal - whose magnitude is
  twice the face area, so larger faces weight more - scattered onto its three
  vertices and then L2-normalised (a zero-area vertex maps to the zero vector, not
  `NaN`). This orientation is what turns an unsigned curvature magnitude into a
  *signed* mean curvature, the sign convention of
  [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-curvature-from-laplacian).

The host-assembled `mesh_cotangent_laplacian(mesh, *, format='ell')` fuses the two
halves - graph adjacency reweighted by the cotangent metric - into the stiffness
operator the next chapter studies in detail.

## Reading Forward

The next chapter specializes this substrate to one of the central operators in
surface geometry: the cotangent Laplace--Beltrami operator. In the notation of
this chapter, `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` constructs a sparse stiffness operator,
`vertex_areas` supplies the mass, and `apply_operator` carries the result into
differentiable algorithms.

The same substrate reappears later in:

- [curvature](curvature.qmd), where cotangent stiffness and vertex mass produce
  mean-curvature normals;
- [spectral geometry](spectral.qmd), where sparse operators enter eigenproblems;
- [volumes](volumes.qmd), where semiring reductions become morphology and
  distance transforms;
- [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd), where mesh adjacency is read as a graph
  operator for pooling, smoothing, and representation learning.

## Further Reading

- Kepner, J., & Gilbert, J. (2011). *Graph Algorithms in the Language of Linear
  Algebra.* The matrix-and-semiring (GraphBLAS) view that "change the algebra, not
  the kernel" formalises.
- Bell, N., & Garland, M. (2009). "Implementing Sparse Matrix-Vector
  Multiplication on Throughput-Oriented Processors." The comparison of sparse
  formats, including ELLPACK-style layouts, that motivates ELL's fixed-shape bet.
- Baccelli, F., Cohen, G., Olsder, G. J., & Quadrat, J.-P. (1992).
  *Synchronization and Linearity.* A foundational reference for max-plus algebra.
- Maclagan, D., & Sturmfels, B. (2015). *Introduction to Tropical Geometry.*
  A broader mathematical view of min-plus and max-plus algebra.
- Veličković, P., et al. (2018). "Graph Attention Networks." The attention
  aggregation that `ell_row_softmax` + `semiring_ell_edge_aggregate` implement.
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds.* The source for
  the mixed-Voronoi area rule used by `vertex_areas`.

## See also

- ELL storage and primitives:
  [`sparse/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py).
- Sectioned (degree-bucketed) ELL:
  [`sparse/ell_sectioned.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py).
- The format-agnostic apply seam:
  [`sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py).
- The algebras, protocols, and reductions:
  [`semiring/algebras.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/algebras.py),
  [`semiring/_types.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_types.py),
  [`semiring/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell.py),
  [`semiring/ell_edge.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell_edge.py),
  and the reference cores
  [`semiring/_reference.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_reference.py).
- The mesh container, icosphere, adjacency, and measures:
  [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py).
- Design records:
  [`sparse-specialisations.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md),
  [`ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md).
- Neighbouring pages: [foundations](foundations.qmd) (execution classes,
  validation, inductive bias) · [Laplace--Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd) (the
  cotangent stiffness and mass) · [volumes](volumes.qmd) (the semiring machine on
  grids) · [performance](performance.qmd#sec-storage) (storage economics) ·
  [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-kernels) (differentiable-kernel depth) ·
  [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd) (pooling and the graph mirror).
